summary: Jeff Atwood recounts Alan Kay's surprising participation on Stack Overflow and shares Jeff Moser's field report from meeting Kay at a computing conference. The post explores Kay's ongoing work at Viewpoints Research Institute, his belief that the 'Computer Revolution Hasn't Happened Yet,' and his critique that we're using computers for distraction rather than becoming better thinkers. Atwood highlights Kay's observations about how programming languages spread through accident and convenience rather than merit, drawing a pointed parallel to PHP. The piece serves as both a tribute to Kay's contributions and a meditation on computing history's lessons for today's developers.
key_points: Alan Kay invented or co-invented object-oriented programming, GUIs, 3D graphics, and contributed to ARPANET's development
Kay believes computers should help us become better thinkers rather than serving as entertainment and distraction
Programming languages often spread through accident and convenience rather than intrinsic merit - Basic displaced the superior Joss simply because it was bundled with GE's timesharing system
Most groundbreaking computing innovations came from the ARPA/PARC era and progress has slowed since
Understanding computing history is essential for understanding the context of inventions and avoiding repeated mistakes
money_quotes: Computers can do so much more than we're using them for now (e.g. provide 'a teacher for every learner').
Our 'pop culture' is more concerned with 'air guitar' and 'Guitar Hero' rather than appreciating genuine beauty and expressiveness of real instruments.
It started spreading Basic around just because it was there, not because it had any intrinsic merits whatsoever.

If there's a big idea and you have deadlines and you have expedience and you have competitors, very likely what you'll do is take a low-pass filter on that idea and implement one part of it and miss what has to be done next.

The adoption of programming languages has very often been somewhat accidental, and the emphasis has very often been on how easy it is to implement the programming language rather than on its actual merits and features.
If you're using early-binding languages as most people do, rather than late-binding languages, then you really start getting locked in to stuff that you've already done.
key_insight: The technologies that dominate our industry often win through historical accident and convenience rather than merit, and we repeatedly fail to realize the full potential of computing's big ideas.
